Types of PEPs

1. Foreign PEPs

  • A natural person who has, or has had at any time, a prominent public function, or who has been elected or appointed to such a function, in a country or territory other than Guernsey.

2. International Organisation PEPs

  • A natural person who is, or has been at any time, entrusted with a prominent function by an international organisation.
    • An international organisation is defined as an entity:
      1. which was established by a formal political agreement between its member states that has the status of an international treaty;
      2. the existence of which is recognised by law in its member states; and
      3. which is not treated as a resident institutional unit of the country in which it is located.
    • It would be appropriate to consider the UN, NATO, World Bank, and similar organisations as international organisations.

3. Domestic PEPs

  • A natural person who has, or has had at any time, a prominent public function, or who has been elected or appointed to such a function, within Guernsey.

Definition of "Prominent"

There is not a regulatory definition of "prominent" with regards to PEPs, but the following are explicitly included:

  • heads of state or heads of government;
  • senior politicians and other important officials of political parties;
  • senior government officials;
  • senior members of the judiciary;
  • senior military officers; and
  • senior executives of state owned body corporates.

Zeta will consider the following to be "senior":

Role Includes
heads of state or heads of government All heads of state or heads of government
senior politicians and other important officials of political parties All elected politicians of a country's highest parliamentary chamber. Elected politicians of lower chambers can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.
senior government officials All ambassadors or equivalent role and all heads of the civil service or equivalent role. Other government officials can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.
senior members of the judiciary All judges and equivalent roles of any level. Zeta may consider not classifying a judge or equivalent role as a PEP if sufficient evidence can be provided of the lower court status of a judge's position, but this consideration should be cautious, and the default position is that any judge or equivalent role is a PEP.
senior military officers All military officers with a NATO standard rank scale of OF-3 or higher. For countries that do not adopt the NATO standard rank scale, the rank should be best matched to the NATO standard rank scale. (NATO Style Guide)
senior executives of state-owned body corporates All executive directors of state-owned enterprises are included. Non-executive directors are by default excluded but can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.

Exclusions

Middle ranking or more junior individuals in the above categories are explicitly excluded from the definition.

Determining "Senior" Status

Where it is unclear if an individual holds, or previously held, a "senior" position, Zeta can consider the following:

  • The role held by the individual;
  • The organisation, government, or other entity at which the individual is employed or affiliated; and
  • The power, responsibilities, and influence associated with the position held by the individual.

Domestic PEPs Reference

A list of roles that qualify as Domestic PEPs is available in Appendix E of the GFSC's Handbook (GFSC Handbook).

Close Family Members and Close Associates

All close family members and close associates of PEPs are considered PEPs. The following are included in those definitions:

Close family members:
  • Spouse or partner held as equivalent to a spouse;
  • Parent;
  • Child;
  • Sibling;
  • Parent-in-law; and
  • Grandchild.

Other family members can be considered as close if sufficient evidence justifies such inclusion.

Close associates:
  • A person who is widely known to maintain a close business relationship with such a person; or
  • A person who is in a position to conduct substantial financial transactions on behalf such a person.

Those persons considered to be close associates could include known partners outside the family unit who would not qualify as close family members (for example, girlfriends, boyfriends and extra-marital partners), prominent members of the same political party, civil organisation, labour or employee union as the PEP, and business partners or associates, especially those that share beneficial ownership of a legal person or legal arrangement with the PEP, or who are otherwise connected (for example, through joint membership of a company board where the PEP and/or close associate is a beneficial owner). Any other persons can be considered as close associates if sufficient evidence justifies such inclusion.

SCHEDULE A: SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION & ANTI-MONEY LAUNDERING REQUIREMENTS

The list of due diligence the Administrator will require can be found in the “CDD Requirements” document provided alongside this form. Guidance on correctly certifying documents, where applicable, can also be found in this document.

The Administrator provides corporate service and administration services for the alternative investment industry. It is the Administrator’s policy that each of its group companies are at all times fully compliant with applicable laws, rules and codes of conduct, and operate in accordance with best industry practices. The Administrator has adopted a risk-based approach with regards to exercising due diligence procedures and implementing AML/CTF controls. The Administrator performs due diligence to verify the true identity of all of its customers, and a list of requirements per category of investor has been summarized below. The level of Customer Due Diligence that is performed on Investors will depend on the risk profile of the Investor.

ANTI-MONEY LAUNDERING REQUIREMENTS - INFORMATION
The Administrator and the Company are subject to the anti-money laundering legislation and regulations and have adopted policies and procedures which use the best practices of international initiatives to counter money laundering and terrorist financing which may be of a standard that is higher than required under anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing legislation (AML Policy). To ensure compliance with applicable statutory requirements relating to anti-money laundering and anti-terrorism initiatives and the AML Policy, the Administrator, on behalf of the Company, will require the identification of any prospective investor and the verification of said identity along with the identification of source of wealth and source of funds from all prospective investors.

The details given above are by way of example only and the Administrator will request such information and documentation as it considers is necessary to identify and verify an applicant and comply with all applicable money laundering regulations and the AML Policy. In the event of delay or failure by the applicant to produce any information required under applicable law or the AML Policy, the Administrator may refuse to accept the application and the subscription monies relating thereto until proper information has been provided. In the event that an investor requests that redemption proceeds be paid to an account in the name of the investor the details of which the Administrator does not already possess, the Administrator may request such written information and documents (which may be required in hard copy) as it reasonably requires to verify such account, and redemption proceeds will not be paid to such account if the investor fails to provide such information. Investors should note specifically that redemption proceeds may not be remitted to an account which is not in the name of the investor.

As mentioned above, the Company, or the Administrator, reserves the right to request such evidence as is necessary to identify and verify a prospective investor in accordance with applicable law or the AML Policy. The Company or the Administrator also reserves the right to request such verification evidence in respect of a transferee of Shares. In the event of delay or failure by the prospective investor or transferee to produce any evidence required under applicable law or the AML Policy, the Company or the Administrator may refuse to accept the application or (as the case may be) to register the relevant transfer, and (in the case of a subscription of Shares) any funds received will be returned without interest to the account from which such funds were originally debited.

If, as a result of any information or other matter which comes to his attention during the course of his business, trade, profession or employment, any Investor or related person (including the Company, its Directors and the Administrator) knows or suspects that payment to the Company (by way of subscription or otherwise) is the proceeds of criminal conduct, such person is required to report such information. Such report shall not be treated as a breach of any restriction upon the disclosure of information imposed by law or otherwise.

By signing this Application Form, the Applicant acknowledges that they will be required to provide the evidence required by applicable law and regulations relating to anti-money laundering checks to prove their identity.

If the Administrator or the Company do not receive satisfactory evidence, then further information may be requested. This Application Form shall not be processed nor shares in the Company issued until such information is received and each of the Administrator and the Company shall be held harmless and indemnified against any loss arising as a result of a failure to process the subscription if such information has been required by the parties referred to and has not been provided by the Applicant.

SCHEDULE E: POLITICALLY EXPOSED PERSONS (INFORMATION)

There are three types of PEPs:
  1. Foreign PEPs
    1. a natural person who has, or has had at any time, a prominent public function, or who has been elected or appointed to such a function, in a country or territory other than Guernsey.
  2. International Organisation PEPs
    1. a natural person who is, or has been at any time, entrusted with a prominent function by an international organisation (such as the UN, NATO, World Bank, and similar organisations).
  3. Domestic PEPs
    1. a natural person who has, or has had at any time, a prominent public function, or who has been elected or appointed to such a function, within Guernsey;
The following are explicitly included in the definition of “prominent” with regards to PEPs:
  • heads of state or heads of government;
  • senior politicians and other important officials of political parties;
  • senior government officials;
  • senior members of the judiciary;
  • senior military officers; and
  • senior executives of state owned body corporates.
The Administrator will consider the following to be “senior”:
Role Includes
heads of state or heads of government All heads of state or heads of government
senior politicians and other important officials of political parties All elected politicians of a country’s highest parliamentary chamber. Elected politicians of lower chambers can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.
senior government officials All ambassadors or equivalent role and all heads of the civil service or equivalent role. Other government officials can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.
senior members of the judiciary All judges and equivalent roles of any level. The Administrator may consider not classifying a judge or equivalent role as a PEP is sufficient evidence can be provided of the lower court status of a judge’s position, but this consideration should be cautious, and the default position is that any judge or equivalent role is a PEP.
senior military officers All military officers with a NATO standard rank scale of OF-3 or higher. For countries that do not adopt the NATO standard rank scale, the rank should be best matched to the NATO standard rank scale 2
senior executives of state-owned body corporates All executive directors of state-owned enterprises are included. Non-executive directors are by default excluded but can be included if sufficient context justifies such inclusion.

Middle ranking or more junior individuals in the above categories are explicitly excluded from the definition.
Where it is unclear if an individual holds, or previously held, a “senior” position, the Administrator can consider the following:
  • The role held by the individual;
  • The organisation, government, or other entity at which the individual is employed or affiliated; and
  • The power, responsibilities, and influence associated with the position held by the individual.
A list of roles that qualify as Domestic PEPs is available on request.
A Foreign PEP or International Organisation PEP does not need to be considered a PEP if the role was ceased to be held more than seven (7) years ago, with the exception of:
  • Heads of state or heads of government or a head of an international organisation; or
  • Individuals that had, during the time of their role, the power to direct the spending of significant sums of money, including:
    • the foreign PEP has/had access to, or authority, control or influence over, significant state assets and funds, policies and/or operations;
    • the foreign PEP has/had control over regulatory approvals, including awarding licences and concessions;
    • the foreign PEP has/had the formal or informal ability to control mechanisms established to prevent and detect ML, TF and/or PF (for example, control over law enforcement or other public sector investigative agencies); and
    • the foreign PEP has/had access to, or authority, control or influence over, the assets of state-owned enterprises.
A Domestic PEP does not need to be considered a PEP is the role was ceased to be held more than five (5) years ago. There are no exceptions to this consideration.
All close family members and close associates of PEPs are considered PEPs. The following are included in those definitions:
  • Close family members:
    • Spouse or partner held as equivalent to a spouse;
    • Parent;
    • Child;
    • Sibling;
    • Parent-in-law; and
    • Grandchild.
    • Other family members can be considered as close if sufficient evidence justifies such inclusion.
  • Close associates:
    • A person who is widely known to maintain a close business relationship with such a person; or
    • A person who is in a position to conduct substantial financial transactions on behalf such a person.
Those persons considered to be close associates could include known partners outside the family unit who would not qualify as close family members (for example, girlfriends, boyfriends and extra-marital partners), prominent members of the same political party, civil organisation, labour or employee union as the PEP, and business partners or associates, especially those that share beneficial ownership of a legal person or legal arrangement with the PEP, or who are otherwise connected (for example, through joint membership of a company board where the PEP and/or close associate is a beneficial owner). Any other persons can be considered as close associates if sufficient evidence justifies such inclusion.

FATCA and CRS Entity Classification Guides

Self-certification is required under the US Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) and the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) Common Reporting Standard (CRS). While the questions and definitions are similar, there are differences which impact some Entities that need to be carefully considered. As a consequence, definitions require careful attention and self-certification under FATCA and CRS needs to be completed independently.

FATCA
FATCA is a legal framework which requires Zeta Fund Services Limited to report details of financial accounts held by US citizens and persons tax resident in the US to the Guernsey Revenue Services on an annual basis who will then exchange this information with the US tax authorities.

FATCA Non-Financial Foreign Entity (NFEE)
Non-Financial Foreign Entity (NFFE): Entities which are non-US Entities and are not Financial Institutions will be regarded as being an NFFE. Each NFFE must be classified as either an Active NFFE or a Passive NFFE

An Active NFFE
An NFFE will be regarded as an Active NFFE if it meets any one of the following:
  1. Less than 50 per cent of the NFFE’s gross income for the preceding calendar year or other appropriate reporting period is passive income and less than 50 per cent of the assets held by the NFFE during the preceding calendar year or other appropriate reporting period are assets that produce or are held for the production of passive income;
  2. The stock of the NFFE is regularly traded on an established securities market or the NFFE is a Related Entity of an Entity the stock of which is traded on an established securities market;
  3. The NFFE is organised in a US Territory, and all of the owners of the payee are bona fide residents of that US Territory;
  4. The NFFE is a non-US government, a government of a US Territory, an international organization, a non-US central bank of issue, or an Entity wholly owned by one or more of the foregoing;
  5. Substantially all of the activities of the NFFE consist of holding (in whole or in part) the outstanding stock of, and providing financing and services to, one or more subsidiaries that engage in trades or businesses other than the business of a Financial Institution, except that an NFFE shall not qualify for this status if the NFFE functions (or holds itself out) as an investment fund, such as a private equity fund, venture capital fund, leveraged buyout fund or any investment vehicle whose purpose is to acquire or fund companies and then hold interests in those companies as capital assets for investment purposes;
  6. The NFFE is not yet operating a business and has no prior operating history, but is investing capital into assets with the intent to operate a business other than that of a Financial Institution; provided, that the NFFE shall not qualify for this exception after the date that is 24 months after the date of the initial organisation of the NFFE;
  7. The NFFE was not a Financial Institution in the past five years, and is in the process of liquidating its assets or is reorganising with the intent to continue or recommence operations in a business other than that of a Financial Institution;
  8. The NFFE primarily engages in financing and hedging transactions with or for Related Entities that are not Financial Institutions, and does not provide financing or hedging services to any Entity that is not a Related Entity, provided that the group of any such Related Entities is primarily engaged in a business other than that of a Financial Institution;
  9. The NFFE meets all of the following requirements:
    1. It is established and maintained in its country of residence exclusively for religious, charitable, scientific, artistic, cultural, or educational purposes;
    2. It is exempt from income tax in its country of residence;
    3. It has no shareholders or members who have a proprietary or beneficial interest in its income or assets;
    4. The applicable laws of the Entity’s country of residence or the Entity’s formation documents do not permit any income or assets of the Entity to be distributed to, or applied for the benefit of, a private person or non-charitable Entity other than pursuant to the conduct of the Entity’s charitable activities, or as payment of reasonable compensation for services rendered, or as payment representing the fair market value of property which the Entity has purchased; and
    5. The applicable laws of the Entity’s country of residence or the Entity’s formation documents require that, upon the Entity’s liquidation or dissolution, all of its assets be distributed to a governmental Entity or other non-profit organization, or escheat to the government of the Entity’s country of residence or any political subdivision thereof.
    6. Any NFFE within the definition of an Excepted NFFE in US Treasury Regulations.
A Passive NFFE
A Passive NFFE is any NFFE that is not:
  1. an Active NFFE or
  2. a Withholding Foreign Partnership, Withholding Foreign Trust, or a Qualified Intermediary pursuant to relevant US Treasury Regulations.
FATCA Financial Institutions
The term “Financial Institution” means a Custodial Institution, a Depository Institution, an Investment Entity or a Specified Insurance Company.

Deemed Compliant Financial Institution
An entity will be deemed compliant if it is listed in the Guernsey – US Inter-Governmental Agreement (IGA) or is within the definition of a Deemed Compliant FFI set out in the US Regulations. There are two categories of deemed compliant institutions – selfcertified or registered.

Certified Deemed Compliant Institutions
  1. Certified Deemed Compliant Financial Institutions listed in the Agreement
    • Non Profit Organisations (Charitable organisations that qualify for exemption from tax in accordance with section 848A and Schedule 26A of the Taxes Consolidation Act 1997, or bodies established for the promotion of athletic or amateur games or sports that have been granted exemption from tax in accordance with section 235 of the Taxes Consolidation Act 1997 by the Revenue Commissioners).
    • Financial Institutions with a local client base which meets all of the conditions set out in Annex II of the IGA, and
    • Certain Collective Investment Vehicles.
  2. Certified Deemed Compliant Financial Institutions listed in the US Regulations
    • Non registering local banks,
    • Financial Institutions with only low value accounts,
    • Sponsored closely held investment vehicles,
    • Limited Life debt investment entities,
    • Owner documented Financial Institutions
  3. Certain Investment advisors and Investment Managers.
Registered Deemed Compliant Financial Institutions
The Financial Institutions falling within this category are not included as Deemed Compliant Financial Institutions under the IGA. However, the institutions are regarded as Registered Deemed Compliant Financial Institutions under the US Regulations. Institutions falling within this category are:
  • Non-reporting members of a group of related Participating Financial Institutions,
  • Restricted funds,
  • Qualified credit card issuers,
  • Sponsored investment entities, or
  • Controlled foreign corporations
Partner Jurisdiction Financial Institution
The term “Partner Jurisdiction Financial Institution” means
  1. any Financial Institution resident in a Partner Jurisdiction (including Guernsey and the UK), but excluding any branches of such Financial Institution that are located outside the Partner Jurisdiction, and
  2. any branch of a Financial Institution not resident in the Partner Jurisdiction, if such branch is located in the Partner Jurisdiction.
Partner Jurisdiction
The term “Partner Jurisdiction” means a jurisdiction that has in effect an agreement with the United States to facilitate the implementation of FATCA.

Participating Financial Institution
A Participating Foreign Financial Institution is a Financial Institution which has entered into an FFI agreement with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) under section 1471(b) of the Internal Revenue Code and § 1.1471-4 of the Treasury Regulations 1 (the FFI agreement) to be treated as a participating FFI.

Exempt Beneficial Owner
The term “Exempt Beneficial Owner” means:
  1. a Governmental Entity;
  2. an International Organisation (examples of which include The International Monetary Fund, The World Bank, The International Bank for Reconstruction and Development and The European Community – for a full list please see the relevant guidance issued by the Guernsey Revenue Service, or the IRS);
  3. a Central Bank;
  4. a Pension trust or other organisations, as referred to in the Double Taxation Treaty between Guernsey and the United States of America, established in Guernsey and maintained exclusively to administer or provide retirement or employee benefits; or
  5. any other entity defined as an Exempt Beneficial Owner in US Treasury Regulations
Non-participating Financial Institution
The term “Non-participating Financial Institution” means a non-participating FFI, as that term is defined in relevant US Treasury Regulations, but does not include a Guernsey Financial Institution or other Partner Jurisdiction Financial Institution other than a Financial Institution identified as a Nonparticipating Financial Institution pursuant to Inter Governmental Agreement.

CRS
CRS is a legal framework which requires Zeta Fund Services Limited to report details of financial accounts held by reportable persons (i.e. customers who are not tax resident in Guernsey or the US) to the Guernsey Revenue Services on an annual basis who will then exchange this information with tax authorities in relevant jurisdictions.

    5. The applicable laws of the NFE’s jurisdiction of residence or the NFE’s formation documents require that, upon the NFE’s liquidation or dissolution, all of its assets be distributed to a Governmental Entity or other non-profit organisation, or escheat to the government of the NFE’s jurisdiction of residence or any political subdivision thereof.
The term Governmental Entity means the government of a jurisdiction, any political subdivision of a jurisdiction (which, for the avoidance of doubt, includes a state, province, county, or municipality), or any wholly owned agency or instrumentality of a jurisdiction or of any one or more of the foregoing (each, a ‘Governmental Entity’).   3. the Entity’s assets vest in one or more Governmental Entities upon dissolution.
  • Income does not inure to the benefit of private persons if such persons are the intended beneficiaries of a governmental programme, and the programme activities are performed for the general public with respect to the common welfare or relate to the administration of some phase of government.   • that is comprised primarily of governments;

The term Corporation, the stock of which is regularly traded on an established securities market or a corporation that is the Related Entity of such a corporation means an entity which is excluded from the definition of a “Reportable Person” which is either:

A Passive NFE
The term Passive NFE means any NFE that is Not an Active NFE.

The term Financial Institution means a Depository Institution, a Custodial Institution, an Investment Entity, or a Specified Insurance Company.
The term Depository Institution means any Entity that accepts deposits in the ordinary course of a banking or similar business.
The term Custodial Institution means any Entity that holds, as a substantial portion of its business, Financial Assets for the account of others. An Entity holds Financial Assets for the account of others as a substantial portion of its business if the Entity’s gross income attributable to the holding of Financial Assets and related financial services equals or exceeds 20% of the Entity’s gross income during the shorter of:
  • the three-year period that ends on 31 December (or the final day of a non-calendar year accounting period) prior to the year in which the determination is being made; or

The term Specified Insurance Company means any Entity that is an insurance company (or the holding company of an insurance company) that issues, or is obligated to make payments with respect to, a Cash Value Insurance Contract or an Annuity Contract.

`; const cfcLetterExplanation = `

Countering Financial Crime Letter

Where a Countering Financial Crime Letter has been requested, this is because Zeta Fund Services Limited ("Zeta") is required to assess your internal controls as part of the onboarding process.

An example of this is a regulated nominee company looking to invest of behalf of its customers. Zeta will not need to conduct any work on those underlying customers (where the nominee company is regulated in an equivalent jurisdiction) as long as Zeta is able to gain comfort that there are sufficient processes in place at the nominee company to onboard those underlying customers.

Required Information

Under Zeta's regulations, the letter must contain information to assure Zeta as to:

  • The entity's risk-grading procedures, including the ability to differentiate between the onboarding requirements for high-risk and low-risk customers; and
  • The entity's application of appropriate and effective due diligence measures in respect of its customers, including enhanced measures for PEPs and other high risk relationships.

Additional Information

In addition, Zeta may request information to assure Zeta as to:

  • The entity's ongoing monitoring of the customers;
  • The entity's use of sanctions and adverse media screening;
  • The entity's training of its staff;
  • The entity's processes as to reporting of suspicions;
  • The entity's application of source of wealth and source of funds controls;
  • The entity's acceptance of so-called 'shell banks' as customers; and
  • The entity's application of a compliance monitoring programme to monitor their ongoing compliance with their local regulations.

Zeta has produced a templated Countering Financial Crime Letter that is available on request.

Certification Process

Certification is an important process through which Zeta gains comfort that the documents it receives are genuine.

When certified documents are received, they must meet certain criteria to be accepted. Zeta reserves the right to reject any document not certified in accordance with this policy.

The Process of Certification

You must arrange to have a copy of the documents certified in the presence of a suitable certifier. The certifier must see the original document and affix specific wording to the copy of the document as well as their details.

Suitable Certifiers

The certification can be completed by any one of the following:

  • Director or Company Secretary of a regulated business in an equivalent jurisdiction (see page 34)
  • Lawyer or Notary
  • Actuary
  • Member of a professional body (e.g. Chartered Accountant)
  • Member of law enforcement
  • Government official or civil servant
  • Director or Company Secretary certifying documents for the Company they represent
  • Authorised signatories of the Customer
  • Zeta employee

When certifying an individual's documents, a certifier cannot be a close family member of the individual.

The Wording of Certifications

For Photo ID documents – I hereby certify that this is a true and accurate copy of the original document that I have seen and the photograph bears a true likeness to the individual who I have met.

For any other documents – I hereby certify that this is a true and accurate copy of the original document that I have seen.

Where web links to company registries or regulators are provided in lieu of documents for entities, Zeta may be able to rely on these without additional certifications. However, in some cases, Zeta may request that the data is extracted and certified. Extracted and certified data will always need to be provided where a database or computer program is used to evidence information (for example, an extract of a register of directors from an internal database). In these cases, the wording must be – I hereby certify this document as a true copy retrieved from an online system, computer program, or external database.

What other information must be included in the certification?

The below must be included in the certification, as a minimum:

  • The signature of the certifier;
  • The full name of the certifier;
  • The professional position or capacity held by the certifier (including professional body membership details where relevant);
  • Details of at least one contact method (for example, postal address, contact telephone number, or e-mail address); and
  • The date the certification was completed.

Zeta reserves the right to contact the certifier of any document to verify their capacity and validate any part of the certification.

`; const ownershipStructure = `

Beneficial Ownership and Control

Under Guernsey's regulations, beneficial ownership comprises a three-step test involving ownership, influence, and management. A Legal Person cannot be onboarded without identifying a beneficial owner at one of the three steps.

Definitions

Term Definition
Natural Person An individual, not a company or other entity
Legal Person An entity, not an individual. For the avoidance of doubt, Legal Person in this context includes, without limitation, legal arrangements such as trusts, foundations, and unincorporated limited partnerships
Customer Zeta's Customer, being the person who is undergoing onboarding
Transparent Legal Person A Legal Person listed on a Recognised Stock Exchange (or a majority-owned subsidiary of such) or a Legal Person regulated in Guernsey
Senior Managing Official An executive director having responsibility for strategy or control over daily affairs of the Customer

Voting & Economic Shares

It is normal for shares in Legal Persons to contain both voting and economic rights. However, some Legal Persons split these and, in these cases, it is important to consider the owners of both types of shares. The definitions are below:

Voting Shares

These shares allow holders to vote on matters raised at shareholder meetings and can include the ability to fire directors or change key operations of the legal person. The shares give a level of control to the shareholder over the legal person even if they do not benefit monetarily from their shareholding.

Economic Shares

These shares allow holders to benefit from dividends from the company as well as proceeds from a liquidation. They will benefit monetarily from the business being conducted with Zeta, even if they do not control the Legal Person.

When disclosing the beneficial owners of Legal Person, beneficial ownership of both voting and ownership shares must be disclosed.

Step 1

A requirement in the onboarding of every Legal Person is the receipt of a certified ownership structure chart showing any holding companies up to and including any natural person beneficial owners having 25% voting or income rights over the limited partnership. This chart will be used in Step 1 of the Beneficial Ownership Test.

The first step concerns ownership of shares or voting rights, namely:

  • Whether any Natural Person or Transparent Legal Person owns 25% or more of the Economic Shares in the Customer;
  • Whether any Natural Person or Transparent Legal Person owns 25% or more of the Voting Shares in the Customer; or
  • Whether any Natural Person or Transparent Legal Person owns any shares in the Customer that carry the right to appoint or remove a majority of directors of the Customer.

If the structure chart shows any Natural Persons fulfilling the ownership criteria (including any holding companies), each such Natural Person should be identified as a beneficial owner.

If the Customer has a Transparent Legal Person in its ownership structure, then the Transparent Legal Person should be regarded as the owner for purposes of beneficial ownership.

A Customer can have more than one beneficial owner. For example, if a Customer only has one class of shares and those shares are held half by a Natural Person and half by a Transparent Legal Person, both the Natural Person and the Transparent Legal Person will be recorded as beneficial owners.

The identification and verification requirements for Natural Persons or Transparent Legal Persons identified as beneficial owners are set out below.

If a Natural Person or a Transparent Legal Person is identified in Step 1, there is no need to move onto Step 2 or 3.

Step 2

This step is relevant where there are reasonable grounds to believe that the Customer is controlled by other means than shareholding or directorship, i.e. where someone identified in Step 1 is significantly influenced by another person or the Customer takes instructions from someone not identified at Step 1. If this occurs, Zeta is required to identify the Natural Person as a beneficial owner.

If a Natural Person is identified in Step 2, there is no need to move onto Step 3.

Step 3

This step (which must be followed if no Natural Persons or Transparent Legal Persons are identified in Steps 1 or 2), requires the identification of any director who meets the definition of Senior Managing Official given as the start of this section. This cannot be a non-executive director and the Senior Managing Official must be a Natural Person. Where a Customer is using Step 3 to identify its beneficial owner and only has corporate directors, this should be discussed with Zeta to identify a suitable Natural Person as beneficial owner.

Identification & Verification Requirements

For any Natural Person identified as a beneficial owner, the requirements are:

  • Certified Photo ID
  • Certified Proof of Address

If the Customer is regulated in an equivalent jurisdiction (see page 34), the documents do not need to be certified.

For any Transparent Legal Person identified as a beneficial owner, the requirements are:

  • Web link as a proof of listing of the Transparent Legal Person (where applicable); or
  • Web link as a proof of regulation in Guernsey of the Transparent Legal Person (where applicable).

Recognised Stock Exchanges

The following are recorded as Recognised Stock Exchanges for the purposes of the Beneficial Ownership Test:

  • any regulated market within the meaning of the European Directive on Markets in Financial Instruments 2004/39/EU;
  • the International Stock Exchange Authority Limited;
  • the Australian Securities Exchange;
  • the New York Stock Exchange;
  • the National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotation System;
  • the Cayman Islands Stock Exchange;
  • the Bermuda Stock Exchange;
  • the Hong Kong Stock Exchange;
  • the Johannesburg Stock Exchange;
  • the SIX Swiss Exchange;
  • the London Stock Exchange Main Market, including the Alternative Investment Market and the Specialist Fund Segment;
  • the Cboe Europe Equities Regulated Market; and

Aquis Stock Exchange Limited.

components.content: Source of Wealth and Source of Funds

components.label: HTML
components.content:
Under Guernsey regulations, we are required to obtain information on an investor’s ‘Source of Wealth’ and ‘Source of Funds’.

A customer’s ‘Source of Wealth’ are the activities that have generated the total net worth of the customer.

Separately, a customer’s ‘Source of Funds’ are the activities that have generated the capital being invested in this onboarding process.

A customer’s ‘Source of Wealth’ and ‘Source of Funds’ may not necessarily be the same.

Please note that one word or vague answers will not be accepted.

components.content: Tax Information

components.label: HTML
components.content:
We are obligated to collect certain information about each entity’s tax arrangements to comply with the Foreign Account Tax Compliant Act (“FATCA”) and Common Reporting Standards (“CRS”). Please complete the sections below as directed and provide any additional information that is requested. Please note that in certain circumstances, we may be legally obliged to share this information and other financial information with respect to the entity’s investments with relevant tax authorities.

If you have any questions about this form or defining the entity’s tax residency status, please consult the entity’s tax advisor or local tax authority.

For further information on FATCA please refer to the following links at www.treasury.gov/resource-center/tax-policy/treaties/Pages/FATCA.aspx and https://www.irs.gov/Businesses/Corporations/FATCAFAQs.For further information on CRS, please refer to the following link at http://www.oecd.org/tax/automatic-exchange.

If any of the information below about the entity’s tax residence or FATCA/CRS classification changes in the future, please advise us promptly of these changes.
